scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill is generally a workspace-generation helper, but it has two notable security concerns: broad capability inflation in its positioning and indirect prompt-injection exposure through referenced files that may not be fully trusted. It also lacks explicit bounds on iterative workflows, which could enable resource exhaustion. No direct code-level exfiltration or command injection was present in the provided content.

meta_correlations.relationship: Both findings describe broad, open-ended agent-building guidance that could increase autonomy or iteration, but they do not identify a concrete malicious mechanism in the actual files.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Constrain the workflow with explicit iteration limits, scope boundaries, and a canonical-reference-only policy for any external reads.

meta_recommendations.title: Add a license field to the manifest
meta_recommendations.fix: Update the manifest metadata to include an explicit license, for example: `license: MIT` (or the correct project license).

meta_recommendations.title: Restrict referenced files to canonical local references only
meta_recommendations.fix: Replace ambiguous references with an explicit allowlist of local files under `references/` and remove any non-canonical or unresolved paths from the instructions.

meta_recommendations.title: Cap iterative refinement loops
meta_recommendations.fix: Add a hard limit such as `max 2 clarification rounds` and `max 1 revision pass unless the user requests more`, then stop and summarize.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: The skill is a benign agent-building guide with one informational metadata issue and no concrete malicious code paths.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Add a license field to the manifest and tighten references to only canonical local files.
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: The content is instructional and does not contain credential theft, exfiltration, command execution, or prompt-injection payloads. The only concrete issue is a missing license field; the other findings are broad-risk observations rather than code-backed threats.
